findings.summary: Component inventory and BOM extraction are accurate; Power rails (GND, Vin, Vout) and net labels correctly extracted; U1 TX4138 pin mapping correctly parsed; U1 power regulator input_rail reported ...

correct.description: All 38 components are correctly identified including references, values, footprints, and types. The count breakdown (7 caps, 3 diodes, 6 fiducials, 9 resistors, 1 IC, 2 LEDs, 1 connector, 4 mounting holes, 1 inductor, 4 'other') matches the schematic. U1 TX4138(ESOIC-8) with its 9-pin symbol is correctly parsed. All BOM entries have the right footprints.

The four named nets Vin, Vout, Vfb, SW, ILIM, GND, and PWR_FLAG are all correctly identified. Net memberships are consistent with the schematic's wire routing and text labels. The Vin label at coordinates (3800,3500), (1600,3500), (1600,2900) all resolve to the same net, as do the Vout labels at (8500,3900), (10300,2900), and (10300,4200).

All 9 pins of U1 are correctly extracted: pin 1 (SW, output) on net SW; pin 2 (ILIM, input) on net GND; pin 3 (Vin, power_in) on an unnamed isolated net __unnamed_8; pin 4 (GND, power_in) on __unnamed_9; pin 5 (FB, input) on __unnamed_7; pin 6 (EN, input) on net ILIM; pin 7 (POK, open_collector) on net Vin; pin 8 (BS, passive) on net Vfb; pin 9 (ILIM, input) on net ILIM. This matches the schematic wiring.

The power_regulators section correctly identifies U1 TX4138 as a switching regulator with inductor L1, output_rail Vout, and notes has_bootstrap: false. The design_observations section correctly flags the missing decoupling cap on the input rail for U1 (the isolated __unnamed_8 pin). The decoupling analysis on Vout (C6+C7, 470 uF + 100 nF) and Vin (C1+C2, 100 uF + 100 nF) is correct.

D1 is correctly classified in protection_devices with protected_net: 'Vin' and clamp_net: 'GND'. SMAJ58A is a 58V transient voltage suppressor diode protecting the Vin rail — this is exactly correct. D2 (SS510 Schottky) is on the SW net serving as the freewheeling diode and is correctly connected between SW (anode) and GND (cathode), though it is not listed as a protection device (appropriate since it is a functional switching element).

Two no-connect markers are present at coordinates (4600, 4100) and (4600, 4000) in the source schematic (lines 1226 and 3225). These correspond to U1 pins at that x-coordinate, which aligns with U1's left-side pins. The analyzer reports total_no_connects: 2, which matches. However, the analyzer reports U1 pin 6 (EN) is on the ILIM net via a wire at (4400, 4100) → (3600, 4100), and the NoConn is at (4600, 4100). This means the EN pin actually has a NoConn on one end and a wire on the other — which in KiCad 5 legacy format means the NoConn is placed near the pin but the pin is actually wired. The two NoConns are on different U1 pins.

The schematic contains a text note: 'When Input Voltage is in the range Vin=8-50VDC, Vout=5VDC/1.5A; When Input Voltage is in range Vin=17-50VDC, Vout=12VDC/1.5A. By default Vout=5VDC. To change to Vout=12VDC, close 12V_E1 jumper.' The analyzer does correctly identify the 12V_E1 jumper as a connector and connects it to the feedback network, but does not extract or report the dual output voltage configuration in the design_observations. This is expected behavior — text notes are not parsed for design intent.

LED_5V1 (green, pin 1 K on __unnamed_10, pin 2 A on GND) with R8 (10K) from Vout to LED cathode is correctly extracted. LED_12V1 (red, K on __unnamed_16, A on GND) with R9 (1K) from the Vout rail to LED cathode is also correct. The Zener D3 (BZV55C6V2) clamping the R9/LED_12V1 node is captured. The LED anode-on-GND orientation means these are reverse-polarity LEDs being driven from the Vout side, consistent with open-drain/POK-style indicators.

incorrect.description: The power_regulators entry for U1 shows input_rail: '__unnamed_8', which is the single-pin stub for U1 pin 3 (Vin power_in). In the actual schematic, U1 pin 3 is not connected via wire to the Vin net — it sits isolated in the schematic at coordinate (4600, 3700) with a NoConn marker nearby. The true Vin supply for U1 enters via pin 7 (POK) which is on the Vin net. However, the larger issue is that __unnamed_8 represents U1's Vin power pin which appears to have no wire connected to it in the schematic — this could be a genuine schematic error (disconnected supply pin) or a wiring that the analyzer failed to trace. The power domain analysis should flag U1 as powered from Vin (via ILIM/EN and the Vin net on pin 7), not from an unnamed rail.

The rc_filters section reports R3 (4.7 ohm) and C4 (100 nF) as an RC filter with input_net: '__unnamed_6', output_net: '__unnamed_7' (going to U1 FB pin), and ground_net: 'SW'. In this buck converter design, SW is the switching node (not ground). The R3-C4 network is a slope compensation / switching noise filter on the feedback signal path, not a standard low-pass RC filter to ground. The 'ground_net' label is misleading — SW is an AC switching node, not a stable reference. The cutoff frequency of 656 kHz is also incorrect due to the wrong parameter parsing (it uses a hardcoded fallback resistance of 402 ohms instead of the actual 4.7 ohm value from the value string).

The voltage_dividers section shows R7 (56.2 K) parsed as 402.0 ohms, R5 (10 K) as 402.0 ohms, and R6 (6.49 K) as 402.0 ohms. Similarly, the rc_filters section shows R3 (4.7 OHM) parsed as 402.0 ohms. The value strings use formats like '0402 56.2 K 1%', '0402 4.7 OHM 5%', '0402 10 K 1%' — the analyzer cannot parse these because the package size ('0402', '0603', '1206') appears first and confuses the value extractor. The fallback 402.0 ohms appears to be extracted from '0402' rather than the actual resistance value. This causes the voltage divider ratio to be reported as 0.5 (equal resistors) instead of the true values: R7/R5 ratio would give Vout = 0.8*(1+56.2K/10K) = 5.3V and with R6 enabled (12V mode) Vout = 0.8*(1+56.2K/(10K||6.49K)) ≈ 12.2V.

The lc_filters entry pairs L1 (33 uH) and C4 (100 nF) with shared_net: 'SW' and reports a resonant frequency of 1.13 MHz. In the TX4138 buck converter, L1 is the main power inductor connected between SW and Vout, while C4 is a small bootstrap/snubber capacitor between SW and GND (connected in parallel with D2 across the switch node). These two components are not part of an intentional LC filter — they are separately functional elements of the switching converter topology. The real output LC filter is formed by L1 (SW to Vout) and C6/C7 (on Vout to GND). The reported L1+C4 pairing is a false positive caused by both having a connection to the SW net.

The voltage_dividers section reports two dividers: R7(top)/R5(bottom) and R6(top)/R5(bottom), both feeding the Vfb node. In the schematic, R6's pin 1 connects to 12V_E1 jumper pin 2, and 12V_E1 is described as 'HN1x2(Opened)' — it is open by default. Therefore R6 is disconnected from the feedback network in normal (5V) operation. The 12V configuration only applies when the jumper is closed. The analyzer does not account for the jumper state and reports the R6 divider as an active network. Additionally, net __unnamed_11 (R6 pin1 / 12V_E1 pin2 junction) is only truly a live net when 12V_E1 is closed; when open, it is a dangling net.

The protection_devices entry for D3 shows protected_net: '__unnamed_17' and clamp_net: 'Vout'. In the schematic, D3 is a 6.2V Zener diode with cathode (K, pin 1) on net __unnamed_17 (R9 output driving LED_12V1 anode) and anode (A, pin 2) on Vout. D3 clamps the LED driver node (__unnamed_17) to Vout+6.2V, acting as an overvoltage clamp on the node connecting R9 and LED_12V1 cathode. The 'clamp_net' should be __unnamed_17 (the node being clamped) and the 'protected_net' would be the reference, but the semantic assignment is reversed. Furthermore, D3 here limits the reverse voltage across LED_12V1 rather than protecting Vout itself.

The feedback_networks section is empty ([]), but U1's FB pin (pin 5) has a clear feedback network: R7 (56.2K) from Vout to Vfb, R5 (10K) from Vfb to GND, and optionally R6 (6.49K) in parallel with R5 via jumper 12V_E1. Additionally C5 (12 pF) is in parallel with R7 for phase compensation. The R3 (4.7 ohm) connects FB to Vfb for slope compensation/filtering. This is a classic switching regulator feedback divider topology that should be detected. The voltage divider detector does find R7/R5 but the result is not promoted into feedback_networks.

In the schematic, C1 (100 uF electrolytic) has transform '1 0 0 -1' (mirror Y) and is placed at P 2900 4500. The '+' pin 1 is reported as being on net GND and the '-' pin 2 on net Vin. For a 100 uF 63V input capacitor between Vin and GND, the '+' pin should be on Vin and '-' on GND. Similarly, C6 (470 uF output cap) has pin 1 (+) on GND and pin 2 (-) on Vout — again reversed. This suggests the analyzer is not accounting for the Y-mirror transform when determining which pin lands on which net, or the pin positions in the component symbol are defined with '+' at the bottom (ground side) for these mirrored electrolytic caps. Either way the polarity is electrically backwards in the output, which would imply a design error if taken at face value.

The decoupling_analysis shows C6 parsed as 0.00047 F (470 uF — correct) and C7 as 6.03e-10 F (603 pF instead of 100 nF = 1e-7 F). The value string '0603 100 NF 100V 10% X7R' results in 6.03e-10 instead of 1e-7. Similarly C4 (0603 100 NF) and C2 (0603 100 NF) both parse to 6.03e-10. This is the same package-prefix parsing problem seen with resistors: the extractor picks up '0603' as 603 and converts to picofarads (6.03e-10 F = 603 pF) instead of parsing '100 NF' = 100 nanofarads = 1e-7 F. The SRF calculations are consequently wrong for these capacitors.

missed.description: The buck converter output filter is L1 (SW→Vout, 33 uH) combined with output capacitors C6 (470 uF, Vout→GND) and C7 (100 nF, Vout→GND). This is the functional output filter with L1 and the Vout-referenced capacitors. The analyzer's lc_filter detection picked up L1+C4 (false) but missed the actual L1+C6 output filter topology. An LC filter with L1 and C6 sharing the Vout/SW interface would be the correct detection.
